The inspection of rooftops plays a critical role in as-
0
5  sessing the structural integrity and maintenance needs of
2  buildings.  Traditionally,  this task has been carried out
:
by inspectors physically accessing rooftops to identify de-
v
i  fects in the building envelope and rooftop equipment. This
X  manual approach is inherently time-consuming, especially
r
for large-scale commercial and industrial structures, and
a
presents significant challenges for visualizing structures.
Advancements in Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle (UAV) tech-
nology and photogrammetry have opened new possibili-
ties for building inspections. UAV’s equipped with high-
resolution cameras can capture images from locations in-
accessible to inspectors, enabling comprehensive docu-
mentation of structures. These images can be processed
using 3D reconstruction techniques to create digital mod-
els of the inspected structures.  This not only facilitates
better visualization of scale and defect locations, but also
serves as a historical record for tracking structural changes
over time.

Infrastructure Assessment

Ontario with labeled roof sections and height above

This study explores the use of autonomous flight paths
for generating accurate 3D reconstructions for building in-
spections. Specifically, it investigates how varying flight
parameters, such as image overlap (OL) and ground sam-
pling distance (GSD), affect the accuracy of the recon-
structed models. The intended outcome of this research
is to be able to optimize these parameters to minimize the
flight duration, computation time, and number of image
captures - key factors for reducing inspection and process-
ing time in commercial applications - while still maintain-
ing accurate models.  There are three primary contribu-
tions of this paper:
• Recommendations based on experimental  testing
for optimal UAV flight parameters in autonomous
photogrammetry-based rooftop 3D reconstruction.
• Validation of a UAV-based LiDAR platform as a
source of ground truthmodels using a high-resolution
terrestrial laser scanner (TLS).
• Application  of  cloud-to-cloud  comparisons  and
quantitative pointcloud analysis for infrastructure as-
sessment applications.

m
Related work was reviewed from two areas of research:
g
(1) Influence factors for 3D reconstruction, and (2) Quan-
l
titative assessment of large-scale mesh models.
c
[
2.1  Influence factors for 3D reconstruction  a
t
Influence factors for UAV photogrammetry-based 3D
b
reconstruction can be broadly grouped into three main
c
categories: flight parameters, camera settings/image ac-
t
quisition,  and reconstruction methods.  While camera
a
settings and reconstruction methods can have significant
impacts on reconstruction quality,  they were out of the
m
scope of this research and were kept constant through-
r
out  the testing.  The influence of flight parameters on
t
photogrammetry reconstruction quality has been inves-
m
tigated thoroughly.  Several studies have confirmed that
s
flight height, image overlap, ground control point (GCP)
p
quantities/distribution, and georeferencing methods have
b
a significant impact on reconstruction accuracy. GCP’s
f
are typically used in larger scale mapping applications
r
such as forestry [1, 2] and glaciology [3], however there
e
has also been research on GCP density and pattern on the
p
reconstruction of dam infrastructure [4].  In this paper’s
o
application of building facade 3D reconstruction, there are
c
enough unique features from the building characteristics
p
that an adequate number of tie points can be achieved dur-
ing feature extraction without the requirement of GCP’s.
Flight height has been investigated individually [5, 6],  3
and in combination with overlap and other parameters
[7, 8, 7, 9, 10, 11]  in various other applications, but
f
less research has been done on how these parameters can
t
influence the reconstruction quality of infrastructure as-
U
sets. Altman et al.  investigated how varying reconstruc-
t
tion methods, number of images, and number of control
T
points (CP’s) could influence the reconstruction quality of
f
a church facade [12]. However, all data collected was from
T
terrestrial sources.
t
o
2.2  Quantitative assessment of mesh models
w
Severalmethods have been established for quantitatively  b
measuring model quality and accuracy.  These methods  t
can be broadly categorized into three areas:  (1) Recon-
structionmetrics, (2) Discretemodel metrics, and (3) Con-  r
tinuous model metrics. Metrics from the reconstruction  d
process such as number of tie points and number of points  b
in the dense cloud have been shown to correlate with lower  o
control point RMSE values [2]. Discrete metrics consist  t
of control points with known locations that can be used
to determine vertical and horizontal error at various loca-
tions on the model. These methods can be used to indicate
drift or misalignment, but they do not provide continuous  O
information about mesh accuracy across the entire model  c

urface.  Continuous model metrics can be divided into
easures of mesh visual quality and measures of global
eometric accuracy. Mesh visual quality metrics such as
ocal roughness, noise, curvature variation, and topology
an be used to quantify the appearance of a mesh surface
13].  Continuous metrics for measuring mesh accuracy
re less common in large-scale 3D reconstruction due to
he requirement of a ground truth model (GT), which can
e difficult to capture at high degrees of coverage and ac-
uracy.  Typically, a GT is captured using TLS systems
hat can produce point clouds at a high degree of accuracy
nd point density [12, 14, 13, 15].
Chen et al. outlined a workflow for the inspection of a
asonry bridge through UAV-based data acquisition, 3D
econstruction, data quality evaluation, and damage detec-
ion [15]. They used a TLS point cloud to provide quality
etrics for five different potential defects in the recon-
tructed photogrammetry model. They acknowledge the
resence of occlusions in their TLSmodel due to obstacle-
locking and self-shadowing. These coverage-based de-
ects would be exacerbated for roof reconstructions, where
ooftop equipment and obstructions would limit the cov-
rage of the TLS system to an even greater extent. In this
aper, a two-stage GT method is proposed. A TLS is used
n the rooftop to capture specific areas of high geometric
omplexity. This is used to validate a UAV-based LiDAR
ayload that collects data across the entire roof structure.

Within the field of photogrammetry, the standard path
or autonomous flights is a double-grid boustrophedon pat-
ern using an oblique camera angle [7]. This involves the
AV making back and forth passes at a specified dis-
ance in perpendicular directions across the coverage area.
his method allows non-horizontal surfaces to be captured
rom four directions and reduces the chance of occlusions.
hese paths can be defined with two flight parameters:
he image overlap (OL), and (b) the GSD. Because of the
blique camera angle and the varying roof elevations, GSD
as used as a variable parameter instead of flight height to
etter represent the distance between the camera and the
arget surface.
GSD refers to the resolution of the captured images
elative to the real-world dimensions of the scene. GSD
epends on the distance between the camera and the object
eing captured (𝑍), the camera focal length ( 𝑓 ) and the size
f a pixel on the sensor (𝑝), which can be determined with
he dimensions of the sensor and the image, i.e.,
𝑍
GSD =  𝑝.  (1)

L considers the area of shared coverage between two
onsecutive images. This is expressed as a percentage of

In this study, the approach in assessing flight parameters
for 3D photogrammetry has three steps: 1. Data Collec-
tion: collected image data from 9 flights of the building
in Figure 1 using a DJI Phantom 4 Pro V2. The research
team collected UAV-based LiDAR data using a DJI Ma-
trice 350 RTK, and TLS data using a FARO Focus.  2.
Model Generation:  generated 3D reconstructions using
photogrammetry software Reality Capture [16]. LiDAR
models are processed in DJI Terra [17], and TLS mod-
els are aligned using SCENE point cloud software [18].
3. Model Evaluation: All models were trimmed, aligned,
and compared using 3D point cloud processing software
CloudCompare [19].
4.1  Data collection
Ellis Hall  on  the  campus  of Queen’s University,
Kingston, Ontario (latitude: 44.226, longitude:  -76.496)
was chosen as the test site for this experiment. The build-
ing features five distinct roof segments at AGL elevations
ranging from 1.71 m to 14.16 m. The roof sections con-
tain a variety of rooftop features, including an observa-
tory dome, various HVAC equipment, fencing and barrier
walls, and experimental testing equipment. This variance
in elevation and subject matter provides unique levels of
obstruction, lighting conditions, GSD, and feature com-
plexity for the experiment.
Images for the photogrammetry reconstructions were
collected using a DJI Phantom 4 Pro V2, a consumer mul-
tirotor UAVwith an on-board 20Mcamera featuring an 84°
field of viewand an 8.8mm focal length, capable of captur-
ing 4k images. The goal of this research was to assess the
photogrammetry reconstruction quality of fine details and
unique rooftop features in real-world conditions. As such,
a process was needed that would provide a continuous,
global metric of model accuracy across the entire mesh
surface.  To do this, A ’ground truth’ model (GT) was
needed to assess the accuracy of the generated reconstruc-
tions. A LiDAR scan of the building was performed using
a DJI Matrice 350 RTK, equipped with a Zenmuse L2
LiDAR sensor. The L2 features a frame-based LiDAR, in-
ternal IMU system, and an RGB mapping camera [20]. A
D-RTK 2 Base Station and on-board RTKModule provide

Real-time Kinematic Processing (RTK) for accurate local-
ization during the flight, and Post Processing Kinematics
(PPK) was done using DJI Terra [17]. The L2 specifica-
tions report a ranging accuracy of 2 cm at 150 m scanning
distance, which is reported to improve at closer ranges.
This paper proposes the use of a point cloud collected
from the Zenmuse L2 as a GT model to provide quantita-
tive evaluations of accuracy for the photogrammetry-based
3D reconstructions.
The UAV-based LiDAR point cloud was validated using
the Faro Focus TLS platform. The TLS was placed in 14
locations across Roof Section B and Roof Section D. The
Faro Focus produces a point cloud with 2 mm accuracy
[21].  Ground control spheres were used to register the
scans inFaro’s SCENE3Dpoint cloud software. Three test
segments of the roof were chosen to compare the TLS and
LiDARpoint cloud’s. Areas of geometric complexitywere
chosen to highlight potential deficiencies in theUAV-based
LiDAR’s effectiveness as a GT model. A cloud-to-cloud
(C2C) comparison was conducted between the two point
clouds at each test segment. One of these comparisons
can be seen in Figure 2. The test segments averaged an
85% F-score at a threshold value of 2 cm.

4.2 Model generation
Photogrammetry models were generated using Real-
ity Capture photogrammetry software [16]. Reality Cap-
ture uses a photogrammetry pipeline consisting of feature
extract,  feature matching, and a Structure-from-Motion
(SfM) algorithm to establish a set of tie points. Ameshing
stage generates a geometric surface for the scene using
a triangulation algorithm.  Finally, a Coloring/Texturing
stage estimates a color for each vertex based on the input
images.
Reality Capture’s settings were kept to their default val-
ues with the exception of the following alignment parame-
ters: the maximum features per megapixel were increased
from 10,000 to 20,000 and the maximum features per im-
age was increased from 40,000 to 100,000.  The prior
hardness of position and the prior hardness of orientation
were decreased from 1.0 to 0.1. Preselector features were

Based on qualitative observations, it was observed that
he flights at 60%and 70%OL producedmodels with large
istortions, as seen inFigure 4a andF-Scores inTable 2. At
hese overlap percentages, even some flat rooftop surfaces
ere not accurately represented by the model. Because
f this observation, these OL percentages where not used
n future testing. Above 80% OL, models began to better
epresent the structure, but there were still variations in
he reconstruction accuracy of fine details and complex
eometry, as seen in Figure 3.
Some clear trends were observed from the C2C quan-
itative analysis conducted. The flights at 0.75 - 1.26 cm
SD generally outperformed the other flights, even when
ompared with flights that had significantly more images
aptured. This was observed for most roof sections: Flight
or Flight 7 had the highest F-score for four of the five roof
ections. Another interesting observation is that Flight 5,
aving captured only 96 images, shared the third highest
verage rank with Flight 4 and Flight 9, which had 249
nd 360 captures, respectively. It also had the lowest over-
ap of the three flights. The analysis also highlights the
eduction in model quality for the lowest rooftop sections,
and E, at a higher GSD. At the same OL, Flight 9 had
n F-Score that was 12.3% lower for Roof E and 15.9%
ower for Roof D, when compared with Flight 7.
The precision and recall were also plotted across dif-
erent threshold values for each of the roof sections.  In
igure 4 and Figure 5, an example of this is shown for
oof sections B, C, and D. It is clear the flights at 0.75 -
.26 cm GSD (gold, turquoise, and pink on the diagram)
onsistently perform well across a range of threshold val-
es. These plots also illustrate the under-performance of
he 0.98 - 1.49 cm GSD flights on Roof D, shown in lime
reen and purple on the graphs in Figure 5 and Figure 6.

This paper provides three additional findings with re-
gard to autonomous flight paths for rooftop 3D recon-
struction:
• For roof sections that don’t exhibit complex geom-
etry, lower OL can still yield effective results while
reducing flight time, image captures, and computa-
tion time. This can be seen in Roof C, where 70%
OL produced a model with an F-score greater than
95%ata4cmthreshold.
• Thin-walledsurfacesandfinedetails(e.g. chain-link
fences, metal bar grating) were not captured accu-
ratelyevenatoptimalOLandGSDvalues. Supple-
mentaryimageswouldneedtobecapturedmanually
aroundthesefeaturesforaccuratereconstruction.

nvelope 3D reconstruction. With added parameters and

The optimal parameters were also tested using other

This paper investigated how varying flight parameters
can influence the reconstruction accuracy of rooftop 3D
models. Through a series of field tests, the study demon-
strated that GSD values of 0.75 - 1.26 cm and an overlap
of 85% provided the most efficient flight path while main-
taining a high degree of reconstruction accuracy. Using a
rooftop TLS configuration, UAV-based LiDAR has been
validated as an effective method for providing accurate
rooftop point clouds that can be used as suitable ground-
truth models for photogrammetry.

This research was supported by Maintenance Drone
Company and Mitacs through the Mitacs Accelerate Pro-
gram. Additional support was provided by the Ingenuity
Labs Research Institute at Queen’s University.
